The performance gains become more noticeable and obvious when using ResNet-50 as the encoder and when fewer labels, e.g. 92 and 182 labels, are available. Our method can still improve the previous SOTA by 1.91% and 1.35% with 1/2 and full fine annotations, respectively. Our AugSeg obtains new SOTA performance of 79.29%, 81.46%, and 80.50% based on ResNet-101, which obtains around 2% performance improvements again previous SOTA.

In this paper, the method proposes AugSeg, a simple-yet-effective approach to semi-supervised semantic segmentation.
AugSeg is a method introduced in the context for semi-supervised semantic segmentation through a standard two-branch teacher-student framework.
The key approach of AugSeg involves training models using a two-branch teacher-student framework on both labeled and unlabeled data.
AugSeg simplifies and revises two existing augmentation models, including random intensity-based augmentation, to improve performance in semi-supervised semantic segmentation.
AugSeg also simplifies and revises adaptive label-aided CutMix-based augmentation to enhance performance in semi-supervised semantic segmentation.
AugSeg achieves state-of-the-art (SOTA) performance on popular semi-supervised semantic segmentation benchmarks without the need for complex designs.
AugSeg demonstrates superior performance on semi-supervised semantic segmentation benchmarks under different partition protocols, indicating its effectiveness in this context.
The authors of AugSeg propose that their method can serve as a strong baseline for future studies in semi-supervised semantic segmentation, implying its potential impact on the field.
